title: How Creators in Cameroon Sell Digital Products Online: Which Option Works Regardless of Where You Are — The Honest Version
author: Unchained Commerce
excerpt: Creators in Nigeria, Pakistan, Ghana, Bangladesh, and dozens of other countries cannot use the platforms everyone else takes for granted. This changes that.

What You Are Actually Comparing

When evaluating options for selling digital products online, it's essential to consider the differences between traditional platforms and crypto-native infrastructure, as the latter offers a more seamless and global experience, allowing creators to focus on their craft rather than navigating complex payment systems, and with the rise of digital payments, it's crucial to understand the nuances of each option to make an informed decision that suits your needs, taking into account factors such as transaction fees, payment processing times, and customer support, all of which can significantly impact your business's bottom line and reputation.

Traditional platforms like PayPal, Stripe, Gumroad, and Payhip often break down for creators in restricted countries due to geographic restrictions, banking limitations, or KYC requirements, resulting in a lack of access to international payments, limited payment options, and a convoluted setup process that can be daunting for those who are not familiar with the intricacies of online payments, and even when these platforms do work, they often come with exorbitant fees, chargebacks, and refunds, which can be devastating for small businesses or individual creators who rely on every sale to make a living, highlighting the need for a more inclusive and accessible payment solution.

Your Launch Checklist — No Platform Permission Required

For a store intended to serve buyers globally, the pre-launch checklist covers both the technical layer and the coin selection layer. Confirm SOL is enabled for buyers who prefer speed. Confirm BTC is enabled for higher-value products. Test at least one complete transaction with USDT and one with a second coin before promoting. A globally-accessible store that accepts only one coin is leaving a significant portion of potential buyers unable to complete a purchase.
